_comment: Masterman Assessment v2.2 — 25 reviewer-approved questions. 5 minutes. IDs preserved from v2.0/v2.1 review docs for traceability (do not renumber). Module 1 has 7 questions, Modules 2–4 have 6 each. Do NOT edit question IDs, axis, module, type, or scoringDirection without updating lib/scoring.ts.

questions.text: I know exactly why Allah put me on this earth, and I can say it in one sentence.
Most weeks, I feel like I am reacting to life instead of leading it.
When my faith is tested, I return to the Qur'an and Sunnah before I return to my own thinking.
If my closest friend described my life in one sentence, it would sound like a man without direction.
I find myself changing how I act depending on who I am around.
In the last 30 days, I have made at least one decision that cost me something because it was the right thing to do.
Your boss or business partner asks you to do something that crosses an Islamic line. Nothing illegal — just wrong. What do you actually do?

options.label: Refuse and explain my reasoning openly.
Push back respectfully and refuse, even if it costs me.
Find a workaround so I don't have to choose.
Do it this once and tell myself I'll fix it later.

questions.text: When I feel anger rising, I can stop myself before I speak.
My phone controls more of my day than I control of it.
In the last 7 days, I broke a small promise I made to myself.
When I am alone and no one is watching, I am mostly the same man I am in public.
In the last week, I lost my temper in a way I later regretted.
It is 11pm. You are tired. You told yourself you would pray Isha and sleep. Your phone is in your hand. What actually happens most nights?

options.label: I put the phone down, pray, and sleep.
I scroll for 10 minutes, then pray and sleep.
I scroll, lose track, and pray late and rushed.
I scroll until I fall asleep and miss Isha.

questions.text: My wife knows I will lead the household even when it is hard.
questions.textHypothetical: When I marry, my wife will know I will lead the household even when it is hard.

questions.text: I avoid hard conversations with my wife and hope they pass.
questions.textHypothetical: When I marry, I expect I will avoid hard conversations with my wife and hope they pass.

questions.text: My wife would say I listen to her without trying to fix, defend, or dismiss.
questions.textHypothetical: When I marry, my wife will say I listen without trying to fix, defend, or dismiss.

questions.text: In the last 30 days, I initiated a conversation about something important at home that I was avoiding.
questions.textHypothetical: When I marry, I will initiate the hard conversations at home, even when I want to avoid them.

questions.text: When I come home stressed, my family feels it before I say anything.
questions.textHypothetical: When I marry, my family will feel my stress before I say anything.

questions.text: Your wife tells you she is unhappy with how things are at home. You do not fully agree. What do you do?
questions.textHypothetical: Imagine your wife tells you she is unhappy with how things are at home. You do not fully agree. What would you do?

options.label: Listen fully, ask questions, then lead a real conversation.
Listen, take a day to reflect, and come back with a plan.
Listen, get defensive, and try to explain my side.
Agree to keep the peace and change nothing.

questions.text: I have at least two Muslim brothers I can call when I am struggling.
Most weeks I go without real conversation with another Muslim man.
There is at least one brother who would correct me if I was wrong, and I would listen.
I tell myself I do not need other men — I just need Allah.
In the last 30 days, I told another Muslim man something I was struggling with, honestly.
You are going through the hardest month of your life. Who actually knows about it?

options.label: Two or three brothers I trust — and we have talked about it.
One brother knows part of it.
Nobody. I am handling it alone.
